title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Food & Agriculture
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Ransomware reaches fairlife’s production systems — Coca-Cola halts US dairy manufacturing
items.content_text: The Coca-Cola Company disclosed on 16 July that a third party gained unauthorised access to part of its fairlife dairy subsidiary’s environment, including its production-related systems, in a ransomware event. US production is temporarily suspended; Canadian operations are unaffected, and the company says product quality and safety are not affected. No group has claimed the attack, Coca-Cola has not said whether data was taken, and it states the full scope, nature and impacts of the incident are not yet known. This is the food and beverage sector’s clearest recent reminder that a plant does not need its PLCs touched to stop: ransomware rarely speaks ICS protocols — it lands on the ERP, MES, virtualisation and identity infrastructure that scheduling, batching, pasteurisation records and quality release depend on, and a perishable-product line halts anyway. How long the outage lasts is decided beforehand by segmentation, credential separation, offline restorable backups, and whether operators can still run the process manually.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: The Coca-Cola Company statement / fairlife / Cybersecurity Dive
_otthreatfeed.date_note: Disclosed 16 Jul 2026 — US production suspended; investigation ongoing

items.title: Sapporo Holdings shuts systems at two overseas subsidiaries after unauthorised access at Pokka and Sleeman Breweries
items.content_text: Sapporo Holdings disclosed unauthorised access affecting two overseas group companies: Singapore-based food and beverage firm Pokka, where access was confirmed on 14 June, and Canadian brewer Sleeman Breweries, confirmed on 17 June. The company detected suspicious network activity, shut down the affected systems and opened an investigation into whether data was taken. Sapporo reported no impact on domestic operations and said the two intrusions showed no established causal relationship at the time of disclosure. No ransomware attribution has been made public and no production impact has been confirmed — treat both as open questions rather than assumptions. The pattern is the familiar one for food and beverage groups: the parent is not breached, an overseas subsidiary is, and the subsidiary was acquired with its own network, its own IT team and its own standards.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Sapporo Holdings / Sapporo Breweries disclosure / The Record
_otthreatfeed.date_note: Access confirmed 14 & 17 Jun 2026 — disclosed 24 Jun; investigation ongoing

items.title: SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days exploited — the remote access into processing plants and cold chain
items.content_text: SonicWall confirmed active exploitation of two Secure Mobile Access 1000 flaws after investigating multiple cases. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface; CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2) lets an authenticated admin inject OS commands through the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July with a 17 July federal deadline. For food and beverage the exposure follows the sector’s shape: processing and packaging sites are geographically dispersed, run lean overnight, and depend on remote support from OEMs for filling, packaging and refrigeration equipment. Cold-chain monitoring in particular is often reached remotely by a third party. When the appliance carrying that access is what gets exploited, the intrusion arrives as a legitimate maintenance session.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: SonicWall advisory & KEV addition 14 Jul 2026 — reporting 15 Jul; ongoing

items.title: Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the boxes bridging older process gear
items.content_text: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038 in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, one of the flaws disclosed in April via the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. In food and beverage plants these devices are how legacy equipment reached the network without being replaced: weigh stations, older fillers and cappers, batch scales, refrigeration and pasteuriser controllers, and CIP skids. They were usually installed by an integrator during a retrofit, never entered in an asset register, and never patched, because the machine behind them still runs. That is what makes the category attractive — unmanaged, unpatched, inside the trusted network, with a direct path to a process asset. Exploitation is confirmed and follows an explicit OT threat warning; for most processors the honest first step is finding them.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Disclosed Apr 2026 — exploitation reported 25 Jun 2026; ongoing

items.title: Context: Food and Ag-ISAC’s 2025 data still frames the sector’s ransomware exposure
items.content_text: Foundational context for the incidents above rather than fresh news: the Food and Ag-ISAC’s ransomware report, published 12 February 2026, recorded 265 attacks against food and agriculture during 2025 — about 4.2% of the 6,377 incidents it tracked across all sectors, itself an 82% rise on the 3,508 tracked in 2024. Five groups — Qilin, Akira, CL0P, Play and Lynx — accounted for close to half of the sector’s recorded incidents. A follow-on sector threat report in March identified 72 active threat actors targeting food supply chains, with observed adversary activity attributed predominantly to Russia (about 59%) and China (about 25%). The numbers are a year old and should be read as baseline, not as this week’s picture.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Ransomware report published 12 Feb 2026; sector threat report 17 Mar 2026 (2025 data)

items.title: July ICS Patch Tuesday: critical Rockwell Logix DoS flaws and a Siemens CVSS 10 auth bypass
items.content_text: The July 2026 cycle carries real weight for food and beverage plants. Rockwell Automation published 12 advisories including three critical denial-of-service flaws in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers that can force a major non-recoverable fault — a controller that stops and does not restart on its own. Allen-Bradley Logix hardware is widespread across filling, packaging and process lines, so this is a throughput and product-loss question, not a data one; on a line running perishable product, an unplanned controller stop has a cost that starts accruing immediately. Siemens published nine advisories, six critical, including a CVSS 10 authentication bypass in Opencenter X and fixes for Simatic S7-1500. Schneider fixed a high-severity code-execution flaw in its IGSS SCADA product. VDE CERT added five more covering Codesys, Wago, Murrelektronik and Mettler Toledo — the last of which is common in weighing and inspection.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Rockwell PSIRT / Siemens ProductCERT / Schneider / VDE CERT / SecurityWeek

items.title: BeyondTrust patches critical auth-bypass flaws in Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access
items.content_text: BeyondTrust disclosed two authentication-bypass vulnerabilities on 7 July affecting Remote Support (RS) and Privileged Remote Access (PRA). CVE-2026-40138 (CVSS 9.2) stems from improper validation of authentication data and could let a network-positioned attacker bypass access controls to reach the appliance, including elevated accounts; CVE-2026-40139 involves improper processing of RS authentication requests. Exploitation of both requires a specific authentication configuration to be enabled, narrowing the exposed population, and neither is under active exploitation — no public proof-of-concept exists. Cloud customers were patched on 21 April; self-hosted RS/PRA at 25.3.2 or earlier needs action. Alongside the SonicWall item, the theme is consistent: the tooling that brokers third-party access into plants is under sustained research pressure.

items.title: Australia’s CISC unveils Enhanced CIRMP Rules covering AI, legacy systems, supply chain and insider risk
items.content_text: Australia’s Cyber and Infrastructure Security Centre published Enhanced CIRMP Rules addressing AI, legacy systems, supply-chain and insider risks across critical infrastructure. Food and grocery sit within Australia’s SOCI critical-infrastructure regime, so processors and retailers with Australian operations have direct exposure here. The broader signal matters to everyone else: legacy systems and supply-chain risk are moving from acknowledged facts of life into written obligations. For a sector where a decades-old retort, filler or blast freezer is often described as unpatchable and left there, an explicit regulatory expectation changes what “accepted risk” has to look like on paper — and those expectations usually reach customer questionnaires long before they reach other jurisdictions’ statute books.

items.title: NIST publishes an OT backup quick-start guide aimed at cutting recovery time
items.content_text: NIST released SP 1339, an OT Backup Quick Start Guide intended to improve industrial cyber resilience and accelerate incident recovery. It lands usefully next to this month’s Rockwell advisories describing faults a controller will not recover from unaided (see Critical Advisories). In a processing plant, what determines how fast you restart is PLC logic, HMI projects, recipe and setpoint data, batch records, drive parameters and the engineering-workstation image needed to push them back — not the file server. Those artefacts are frequently stale, undocumented, or sitting on the same domain as the systems they are supposed to restore. In food specifically there is a second clock running: extended downtime is not just lost output, it is product at risk and, potentially, a traceability gap.
